title: The Board Resolves
synopsis: Maps the emerging tripartite structure of global power: Musk's vertically integrated energy-compute-finance stack, Google's quietly indispensable Western infrastructure, and China's sovereign parallel civilisation, with orbital access as the unclaimed territory that may decide the age of abundant intelligence.
preview: The trajectories are measurable. I want to start there, because what follows will sound like speculation, and some of it is, but the foundation is ...
content:
The trajectories are measurable. I want to start there, because what follows will sound like speculation, and some of it is, but the foundation is not. The cost curves for AI compute, solar energy, and robotics are empirical. They are published quarterly. They move in one direction. And if you take them seriously, if you follow them forward even a few years with a pencil and some honesty, you arrive at a picture of the world that most people are not yet willing to look at directly.

I am going to try to look at it directly. I will get parts of it wrong. But I think the shape is legible, and I think the discomfort we feel when we try to describe it comes not from its implausibility but from how thoroughly it challenges the assumptions most of us built our lives around.

Start with intelligence, because intelligence is the solvent that dissolves everything else.

The capabilities of frontier AI models are improving at a rate that, if anything, has been accelerating. Benchmarks fall quarterly. Context windows expand. Reasoning sharpens. The cost per token collapses. I am not going to claim I know the precise moment these systems cross whatever threshold we eventually agree to call general intelligence. Nobody knows that. But the curves do not suggest decades. They suggest proximity. Perhaps something that looks like early AGI by mid 2026. Perhaps something that no longer requires the qualifier by late 2027.

I hold that loosely. But I hold it.

And once you hold it, even loosely, everything downstream rearranges. Because general intelligence dissolves the constraints every other industry and institution was built around. Labour scarcity. Cognitive bottlenecks. The assumption that knowledge work requires human minds. Once those go, the architecture of the world has to be rebuilt. The question becomes: by whom, and on what foundation?

I think the board resolves into three.

Consider what has happened in just the last few months.

SpaceX absorbed xAI in February, valuing the combined entity at roughly $1.25 trillion. Musk confirmed the IPO for mid 2026, with reports suggesting a target valuation around $1.75 trillion. The stated purpose of the capital raise, written plainly in federal filings, is to develop orbital data centres: modified Starlink satellites that generate and consume compute in space, powered by uninterrupted solar energy. The filing language referenced Kardashev Level 2 civilisation. Federal paperwork, not my editorialising.

Meanwhile, Wedbush's Daniel Ives has been saying publicly what the structure has been whispering for a while now: that Tesla may follow. That consolidation is the logical terminus of Musk's architecture. And you can see why, if you lay the pieces out.

Tesla produces solar panels, battery storage, electric vehicles, autonomous driving software, humanoid robots, and AI training chips. SpaceX produces launch infrastructure, satellite internet, and potentially the largest energy generation capacity in history. xAI produces the intelligence layer. These are subsystems of a single vertical stack wearing different corporate clothes. Once unified under one equity structure, the compounding begins in earnest.

X Money launched in limited beta this month. Six percent yield on deposits. Visa partnership. Licensed in over forty American states. Peer to peer transfers, debit cards, direct deposit. Musk's stated ambition, in his own words, is that when something involves money, it will happen on this platform. He has been saying versions of this since X.com in 1999. The difference now is that the platform sits atop an energy and manufacturing stack that gives the financial layer something to actually denominate.

I want to be careful. The gap between ambition and execution remains real, and Musk has a long history of timelines that slip by years. X Money may fail to achieve adoption. The Tesla merger may not happen. The orbital data centres may prove uneconomical at scale. I am not claiming inevitability. I am tracing the trajectory, and the trajectory points somewhere specific: one interconnected entity controlling energy production, energy storage, physical labour, cognitive labour, compute infrastructure, communications, financial infrastructure, and orbital access. Simultaneously. Under one corporate structure.

The East India Company comes closest as a historical analogy, and even that flatters the past. The East India Company operated within the constraints of its era. The entity I am describing would operate in an era where most of the old constraints have been dissolved by the very intelligence it helped create.

Now zoom out.

Google requires a different kind of attention, because Google's power does not announce itself.

Musk builds things you can photograph. Rockets on landing pads. Robots walking across stages. Google builds things you cannot see until you try to remove them, at which point you discover they are load-bearing. The advertising business is the visible revenue. Underneath it runs infrastructure so deeply woven into the daily function of Western economies that extracting it would be roughly as straightforward as extracting the electrical grid. Cloud services hosting a significant share of the internet. The Android operating system on three billion devices. The mapping layer that logistics and navigation depend on. Gmail, which handles a quarter of all email globally. And DeepMind, the AI research lab that arguably produced the most important scientific breakthroughs of the last decade, from protein folding to weather prediction to the transformer architecture the entire AI industry now runs on.

This matters because when general intelligence arrives, it does not arrive into a vacuum. It arrives into existing infrastructure. And the question of who benefits most from abundant intelligence is largely a question of who already has the pipes to deploy it at civilisational scale. Google does. The talent is there. The data is flowing. What changes is that the intelligence animating all of it gets dramatically better, and every dependency that already exists deepens further.

Western governments will grumble about this. They already do. Regulators will posture. But you cannot regulate away a dependency you spent two decades building. You can slow it down, tax it, wrap it in compliance frameworks. You cannot replace it within the timeframe that matters, because the replacement would take longer to build than the transformation will take to arrive.

So Google holds the West. Through the quiet, compounding accumulation of indispensability. The way the telephone network held the twentieth century, or the way the Roman road system held the provinces. You could theoretically build an alternative. You simply would not, because by the time you finished, the world would have moved on.

China has been preparing for this moment with a deliberateness that Western commentary consistently underestimates, partly because that commentary tends to frame China's current economic difficulties as structural weakness rather than what they more likely represent: the turbulence of a managed transition.

The Great Firewall was never merely a censorship apparatus. Viewed from a structural economic perspective, it may be the most effective protectionist policy in the history of digital commerce. It forced the creation of a parallel technology stack, and that stack is now mature. Baidu, Alibaba, Tencent, Huawei, ByteDance. Each mirrors a Western counterpart, but each operates within an ecosystem that has no dependency on American platforms, American cloud infrastructure, or American financial rails. When people talk about decoupling as a future risk, they are describing something China substantially completed years ago.

The energy position reinforces this. China manufactures roughly eighty percent of the world's solar panels and holds dominant positions in the critical minerals the entire energy transition physically depends on. Domestic oil demand has already peaked. The EV transition is further advanced than in any other major economy. Meanwhile, the financial architecture for a post-dollar trading bloc already exists and carries real volume: CIPS processes trillions of yuan daily, and the vast majority of trade within the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation now settles in local currencies.

The AI race between the US and China is misunderstood as a sprint. It is better understood as two civilisations each building sufficient intelligence to operate their respective spheres autonomously. China does not need to beat Google to general intelligence. China needs to reach a threshold of capability that makes its own infrastructure self-sustaining, and it is closer to that threshold than most Western analysts are willing to admit.

If the petrodollar system continues to weaken, and I have argued elsewhere that the current war in the Gulf is accelerating exactly that, the bifurcation deepens. The dollar buys less leverage. American tech sanctions lose teeth as Chinese domestic supply chains mature. And the world sorts itself, quietly, into two digital civilisations that share a planet but increasingly share very little else.

Which leaves space. And I have to be honest: this is where the ground gets softer underfoot, where the argument moves from extrapolation toward something closer to intuition. But the logic is coherent, so let me trace it.

Earth is finite. Not in the sentimental way, though that is also true. Finite in the economic sense. There exists a ceiling to terrestrial energy production, a ceiling to terrestrial compute, a ceiling to the number of data centres you can build before you exhaust land, water, cooling capacity, and the patience of the communities that host them. If intelligence truly becomes the dominant economic input, and if intelligence scales with compute, and compute scales with energy, then the binding constraint on growth eventually becomes the binding constraint on energy. And that constraint, on the surface of the Earth, has a hard limit.

Orbit does not. Solar energy in space is roughly thirteen times more efficient than on the ground. No atmosphere. No weather. No night, if you position the constellation correctly. The filing SpaceX submitted to the FCC proposed up to one million orbital data centre satellites, with the stated rationale that within a few years the lowest cost to generate AI compute would be in space.

If that proves even approximately true, then whoever controls orbital access controls the next energy frontier. And whoever controls the next energy frontier occupies a position in the emerging economy analogous to the one central banks occupy today.

Musk has said, more than once, that his goal is to make humanity multiplanetary. Most people hear aspiration. Eccentricity. The kind of thing billionaires say at TED talks while the audience smiles politely. I have started to hear it differently. I have started to hear a business plan that happens to also be a civilisational project. The Starship programme is the enabling infrastructure for orbital data centres, which fund lunar manufacturing, which funds Mars colonisation. Each step requires the previous step, and the first step, cheap reusable heavy lift launch, already works. SpaceX launches a rocket every two days. Ten thousand Starlink satellites are already in orbit. The infrastructure to claim the territory above the Earth exists today, and nobody else is close enough for the gap to matter on the relevant timescale.

Google secures the West through embedded infrastructure. China secures the East through sovereign technology and parallel trade architecture. And the remaining unclaimed territory, the territory that may determine who commands the age of abundant intelligence, sits on no terrestrial map.

I should pause and acknowledge what I am doing. I am constructing a narrative. Narratives are seductive precisely because they compress complexity into coherence, and the world is under no obligation to cooperate. Google could be broken up by regulators with more spine than I am giving them credit for. China's economy could stall in ways that undermine its technological ambitions rather than catalyse them. Musk's architecture could fracture under its own contradictions, or under the weight of Musk himself, who remains, for all his structural genius, among the most erratic human beings on the public stage.

But the incentive structures point this way. The capital flows point this way. The physics of energy and compute point this way. And the timeline, which always felt safely distant, has been collapsing. The SpaceX IPO is months away. X Money launches next month. The Tesla merger is discussed openly by mainstream analysts. AGI benchmarks are falling faster than anyone projected five years ago. These are no longer forecasts. They are calendar items.

I think about Arthur when I write things like this. I always do. He is too young to understand any of it, and part of me envies that, the part that remembers what it felt like when the future was just a feeling rather than a series of converging data points.

But there is another kind of envy too, and I want to be honest about it.

The world Arthur will grow up in, if even a fraction of this trajectory holds, is the world I dreamed about as a child. I grew up reading science fiction. Watching it. Drawing it in the margins of schoolbooks. Orbital stations. Intelligent machines. Robots that could build things. Energy too cheap to hoard. Those were fantasies then, separated from reality by what felt like centuries of incremental progress, accessible only through paperback novels with cracked spines read on a carpet in Adelaide while the real world outside the window remained stubbornly, defiantly mundane.

Arthur will not experience them as fantasies. He will experience them as Tuesday. As the way things are. As unremarkable.

And I find that I am jealous of him for this, which is a strange thing for a father to admit. I wanted to live in that world. I spent decades imagining it. Now it is arriving, and I am old enough to recognise what is happening but young enough to watch my son walk into it as though it were always there. He will never know the gap between the dreaming and the having. He will never understand what it felt like to want this future from the far side of impossibility, to carry it around as a private suspicion that the world would probably never confirm. He will simply grow up inside the thing that used to be science fiction, and he will find it ordinary. Which is perhaps the greatest gift a generation can give to the next: a world so transformed that the transformation itself becomes invisible.

He will simply grow up inside the thing that used to be science fiction, and he will find it ordinary.

I do not know if this is the world he will inherit. I suspect it might be. I hold that suspicion loosely, as I hold all my suspicions, because the future has a long history of humiliating anyone who claims to know its face.

But the pattern is visible. And it rhymes with patterns I have read about in other contexts.

I emphasise as always that it would be prudent, I think, to pay attention.
